text: Natural orifice transluminal extraction colectomy is an advanced method to perform a colectomy with no abdominal incision and delivery of the specimen through a natural orifice. It is the progression from open disabling surgery to minimally invasive surgery. And now, one step ahead, no abdominal incision surgery. The potential benefits of this procedure include reduction in postoperative pain and wound complication, faster recovery of bowel function, shorter length of stay and better consummation and psychological effects.

Despite these significant advantages, the high level of surgical skills required and the potential pitfalls have contributed so that the technique has not yet been widely adopted. In our knowledge, we present you the first robotic no-tech in Europe with modification of the technique that has been previously described, eliminating the bacteriological and oncological concerns and optimizing operative time. This is a case of a fifty-one year old woman with non-navarticular disease that caused her many admissions to the hospital, so we decided to perform a no-texigmoid resection. As standard, we use our three-D models for precise location of the pathology and understanding of the anatomic variations of the blood supply.

The surgeon begins medial to lateral mobilization, identifying the inferior mesenteric artery. The left ureter is carefully identified and protected. The left colic artery is preserved to ensure optimal blood supply to the colon. The inferior mesenteric artery is clipped after the origin of the left colic artery and then stapled. Medial mobilization is completed, followed by lateral mobilization.

The surgeon dissects adhesions between the uterus and left ovary, followed by mobilization of the upper rectum in the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane. The rectum is prepared for stapling using an Echelon stapler with green cartridges. The Firefly fluorescence imaging of the da Vinci system confirms good blood supply to the rectal stump, indicated by its green coloration. The rectum is then stapled, and the staple line is cut with scissors.

The surgical team removes the specimen from the abdomen through the anus. The anvil is placed, and a purse string suture is applied outside the abdomen. The bowel is returned to the abdominal cavity, and the injured rectal cuff is excised. A 31mm circular stapler is inserted through the anus to create an end-to-end colorectal anastomosis.

A leak test is performed and the operation is over. The patient went home day one with no analgesia and her abdomen was as seen in the picture. Summarizing, the benefits of the technique are tremendous. Patients have less pain, less postoperative complications, reduction of length of stay, less ileus, no hernias, less surgical site infections, no wound complications and better cosmesis and psychological effects. In experienced hands, this is definitely the next step to minimally invasive surgery.
